result:
I keep thinking about the moment after the rain stops—the asphalt still hissing, the gutters sighing like they’ve carried too many small disappointments for too long. The air smells of soaked concrete and heated iron railings, the way childhood kitchens smell after the toast has popped. Everything is raw and over-bright, the way color returns when you adjust the contrast on an old television you’d almost forgotten how to tune.

I used to chase that post-storm moment with the urgency of someone glimpsing the answer just beyond the edge of a dream. Puddles became handheld mirrors until a bicycle tire shattered them. Then the pieces reassembled, the sky stitched itself back together upside-down, and nothing really changed except that I had saved a sliver of reflected light in my memory. I stored it in the same pocket that held my mother’s vanished grocery lists and the name of a friend who moved away without saying goodbye.

We think memory is storage, but it’s actually erosion. Each retrieval scrapes off a translucent layer, like wearing a river stone between finger and thumb for too long. I return to grade-school fire drills, the smell of mimeographed sheets, the sound of 30 small Velcro shoes ripping away from classroom tiles. Every time I fish the memory out, the edges are rounder, the burnished center shinier. The emergency exit sign my six-year-old self once feared is now just a soft red animal asleep above the doorframe.

Maybe that’s why humans invented writing: an external skeleton for memory, calcium for the exoskeleton of mind. I write so my future self won’t believe the past disappeared—that somewhere in the hour after rainfall I really did step over a cluster of ants carrying crumbs in a straight, impossible line, looking like chariots racing the same marble. But even these sentences oxidize. Read what I wrote five years ago and I no longer recognize the person who was certain that ants operated on moral metaphors, or that thunder mocked us from the ridge like a disappointed god.

Tonight the city outside my window has a heartbeat made of car alarms descending in minor thirds, laid over a static pulse of construction lights flickering just above human seizure threshold. Somewhere nearby a group of students performs a thin, jagged laughter that slices open the night long enough for loneliness to leak in. I could close the curtains, but I don’t. There’s harmony in the imperfect chord: loneliness plus rain memory plus the faint citrus scent of tonight’s takeout cooling on the table.

I think we collect these fragments—sound, smell, color—because the whole is too vast to love without the risk of drowning. A single blue bead on a broken bracelet, once plucked from a municipal sandbox in 1993, still sits in a little ceramic dish on my shelf. It is the edge of a universe I will never see again. Yet holding it feels more honest than any star chart ever could.

So I write in spirals, chasing the brightness just after the storm, trusting that every orbit—even when it scrapes—adds a fresh groove to my internal river stone. One day the stone will be so smooth and small I might mistake it for a seed. And perhaps that is the loveliest conversion of memory: erosion eroding until nothing remains except the will to plant something whose leaves have never existed yet.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
